title: How Does a Computer Think? The PB&J Sandwich Lesson
description: Students discover that computers follow exact step-by-step instructions, and that sequence determines the result.
learningObjectives: Explain in their own words that a computer follows instructions exactly, one step at a time.
Describe why sequence matters by showing how changing the order changes the outcome.
Use key vocabulary correctly: algorithm, input, output, and sequence.
Debug unclear instructions by testing and improving a written algorithm.
materials: Bread, peanut butter, and jam (or printed ingredient cards for allergy-safe version)
Optional: printed command cards for shy students
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Students experience computational thinking through a literal robot activity and a PB&J challenge. The class learns that computers do not guess, every step must be explicit, and order drives output. Role model of the day: Ada Lovelace, who wrote the first algorithm designed for a machine long before modern computers.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson: Algorithm, Input, Output, Sequence. Place PB&J materials (or picture cards) at the front table. Prepare paper for pair and individual algorithm writing.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question. Ask: 'What could a robot do better than a human, and what could it never do?' Students share in pairs, then collect 3-4 class answers without correcting.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Unplugged warm-up (Robot Teacher). Allow only four commands: STEP FORWARD, TURN LEFT, TURN RIGHT, STOP. Follow instructions literally and discuss why precision was hard.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro. Define algorithm and explain input, output, sequence. Use real examples (recipe, morning routine, LEGO) and connect to GPS/recommendation mistakes.

Phase 4 (20-42 min): PB&J robot challenge. Round 1 class gives instructions while teacher executes literally. Round 2 pairs write a numbered precise algorithm. Round 3 test one pair algorithm and debug in real time.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion. Use at least one reflection question about responsibility for errors and revisit opening robot-vs-human ideas.
Exercises (47-57 min): Individual written algorithm (minimum 6 numbered steps, one action per step, robot-readable) plus optional sequence sorting game for tea-making steps.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share one step, class spots vagueness, and each student completes: 'An algorithm needs to be ____ because computers cannot ____.'
discussionPrompts: A robot followed your instructions exactly and got the wrong result. Whose fault is that: robot or programmer? Why?
When a GPS gives a technically valid but strange route, is that a robot problem or an algorithm design problem?
Return to the opening question: what can robots do better than humans, and what can they never do? Did your view change?
Is there always one correct sequence, or can some algorithms allow multiple valid orders?
tips: Be extremely literal during robot activities. Misinterpretations are the teaching moment.
Do not correct student opening ideas too early; let understanding emerge through activity.
If students are shy, let them write commands on cards and pass them one at a time.
If allergies are a concern, use printed ingredient cards or substitute with paper airplane, block stacking, or shape drawing.
Watch for self-correction and peer debate about precision; these are key understanding indicators for readiness for Week 2.

teacherGuide: Core message: computers execute instructions exactly, without common sense. Keep vocabulary visible all lesson. Use humor and literal execution during PB&J to surface hidden assumptions, then move into debugging. Prioritize depth over speed in discussion: one strong reflection is better than rushed coverage. Post-lesson, check whether students independently improved vague steps and used precision language correctly.

studentExercises.title: Write the Perfect Algorithm
studentExercises.description: Individually write a numbered algorithm (at least 6 steps, one action per step) for one task: getting ready for school, making chocolate milk, or feeding a pet. Then swap and debug with a partner.
instructions: Choose one task: getting ready for school, making chocolate milk, or feeding a pet.
Write at least 6 numbered steps.
Each line must contain one action only.
Make every step precise enough for a robot that cannot guess.
Swap with a partner and mark unclear steps with a question mark.
Improve unclear steps and submit your final algorithm.
successCriteria: Algorithm has 6 or more numbered steps.
Each step is one clear action.
A partner can follow the algorithm without guessing.

studentExercises.title: Sequence Sorting Game: Make Tea
studentExercises.description: Order six shuffled instruction cards into a logical sequence, compare with a partner, and discuss where sequence is fixed versus flexible.
materials: Printed instruction cards or board projection
instructions: Read the six tea-making instruction cards.
Write the order numbers 1 through 6 next to each step.
Compare your order with a partner.
If you disagree, explain why your sequence works.
Discuss which steps are fixed and which can be flexible.
successCriteria: Students can justify their sequence choices.
Students identify at least one flexible step.
Students connect sequence changes to output changes.

studentExercises.title: Extension: Robot Chef Algorithm
studentExercises.description: For fast finishers: write a canteen robot algorithm for one lunch item, then extend it with allergy safety and missing-ingredient handling.
instructions: Choose one lunch item from the school canteen menu.
Write a precise step-by-step algorithm a robot chef could follow.
Add safety instructions for students with nut allergies.
Add a rule for what the robot should do if an ingredient runs out.
Challenge: make your algorithm work for any sandwich type.
successCriteria: Safety and missing ingredient edge cases are included.
Student can explain how to generalize the algorithm.

studentChallenges.title: Allergy-Safe Robot Chef
studentChallenges.description: Design a robot-chef algorithm that safely handles a nut allergy without using common sense.
instructions: Choose one canteen food item.
Write a step-by-step robot algorithm for preparing it.
Add explicit allergy checks before handling ingredients.
Include safe fallback steps if risky ingredients appear.
Test your algorithm by having another group try to follow it literally.
successCriteria: Another group can follow it without guessing.
Team can explain why each safety step is needed.

studentChallenges.title: What If an Ingredient Runs Out?
studentChallenges.description: Improve your algorithm with a decision path for missing ingredients during preparation.
instructions: Take an algorithm you already wrote.
Pick one ingredient that could run out halfway.
Add precise steps for what the robot does next.
Include at least one IF style instruction in plain language.
Run a peer test to see whether the updated logic works.
successCriteria: Algorithm has a clear missing-ingredient response.
Decision path is precise and testable.
Peer tester can execute both normal and missing-ingredient scenarios.

studentChallenges.title: One Algorithm for Any Sandwich
studentChallenges.description: Generalize from one sandwich to a reusable algorithm that works with different inputs.
instructions: Rewrite your sandwich algorithm so it does not mention only peanut butter and jam.
Use placeholders like SPREAD A and SPREAD B.
Define input choices at the top (bread type, spread 1, spread 2).
Keep the action steps specific and in order.
Test with two different ingredient combinations.
successCriteria: Algorithm can be reused with different sandwich inputs.
Order of steps remains valid across both tests.
Team explains how input changes but algorithm structure stays stable.
